text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Menon  
Riverside Medical Practice  
14-18 Riverside Way, London SE10 8RF  

Dear Dr Priya Menon,  

Alex Porter, DOB: 07-09-1988, Hospital No: U904317 NHS No. 3812047765  
Flat 3, 22 Brookbank Road, London SE13 7QR 

Summary/ Diagnoses: History of seizures with prior head injury in youth/learning difficulties/mild depression (self-reported, under GP review)/vitamin D insufficiency (on supplementation)/former smoker (quit 2019)/no alcohol use. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Sertraline 50 mg once daily.  
Cholecalciferol 800 IU once daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily. 

I reviewed Alex in clinic today, accompanied by his partner who helps with day-to-day organisation and maintains a seizure diary on a mobile app. He has been sleeping better since adjusting his work shifts to daytime only and is using phone reminders for medication adherence. Appetite is stable and weight has increased by 1.2 kg over six months. Neurologically, speech and language are fluent, cranial nerves intact, power full in all limbs, no cerebellar signs, and gait narrow-based but steady. No new injuries reported. 

Seizures: The diary and partner’s collateral history indicate that Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are rare, typically 3 events per year. focal epileptic spasm occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings. Triggers appear to include missed evening doses and late nights; stress at work has also been noted to precede clusters of brief events. There is no consistent aura reported. Post-ictal confusion after major events lasts 30–60 minutes without tongue-biting or incontinence in the most recent episodes. No emergency department attendances since last review. 

Investigations: Routine bloods from August 2025 normal including U&E, LFT, FBC; vitamin D borderline low at 22 nmol/L (supplementation ongoing). MRI brain (2018) showed minor non-specific white matter changes only. No recent EEG available; prior routine EEG (2019) was non-diagnostic. 

Discussion: We reviewed adherence strategies and sleep hygiene. Alex prefers to avoid additional medication at present provided safety measures continue. Driving advice re-iterated as per DVLA standards given intermittent events; he confirms he is not currently driving. Workplace has arranged flexible breaks, which he finds helpful. 

Plan: 
- Continue levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily and lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily without change.  
- Reinforce evening routine and dose reminders; aim for consistent sleep schedule.  
- Provide seizure action plan to patient and partner; no rescue medication requested at this time.  
- Community epilepsy nurse to review diary trends in three months.  
- Arrange sleep-deprivation EEG within the next 3–4 months to reassess interictal activity.  
- Recheck vitamin D level in primary care in 3 months and continue cholecalciferol.  
- Safety advice reviewed (showers not baths, avoid heights/open water when alone, kitchen precautions).  
- Follow-up in clinic in 6 months, earlier if seizure frequency or severity increases. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: focal epileptic spasm occur several times each week
Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are rare, typically 3 events per year. focal epileptic spasm occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure frequencies in the letter. Two types mentioned: 1) Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are 'rare, typically 3 events per year' → 3 per year. 2) 'focal epileptic spasm occur several times each week' → this is a higher frequency but vague; exact number not specified. According to the rule for multiple frequencies, select the highest frequency. Convert 'several times each week' to the allowed format using 'multiple' when exact number cannot be determined. Therefore, final label is 'multiple per week'.
seizure_frequency_number: focal epileptic spasm occur several times each week
reference: Generalised tonic-clonic seizures are rare, typically 3 events per year. focal epileptic spasm occur several times each week, particularly in the evenings.
